coredata.dc:title: Creatine-based carbon dots with room-temperature phosphorescence employed for the dual-channel detection of warfarin
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Miao
Feng, Zhiying
Wang, Jing
Zhu, Ying
Gan, Lanlan
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have harvested enormous attention owing to their advanced optical properties, providing their possibility of the wider applications. Here, we designed a facile strategy to synthesize phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots, while creatine provided the carbon and nitrogen source (CDs@creatine). Meanwhile, the as-prepared CDs@creatine exhibited the bright blue fluorescence in aqueous suspension, and showed the green RTP while being fixed on the filter paper. Significantly, the fluorescence of CDs@creatine was quenched by the introduction of warfarin, whereas their RTP was simultaneously enhanced. Specifically, their fluorescence reduction was mainly ascribed to static quenching effect (SQE) and inner filter effect (IFE). In addition, the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs@creatine interacted with the carbonyl groups of warfarin to form hydrogen bonds which rigidified the excited triplet against the non-radiative transition, and thus leading to their enhanced phosphorescence. Therefore, a dual-channel detecting strategy of warfarin has been successfully proposed with the fluorescent linear range from 8 × 10−3 to 8 × 10−7 M and phosphorescent linear range of 2 × 10−5 to 1 × 10−8 M, which established a new way of assaying warfarin. Besides, CDs@creatine served as both fluorescent and phosphorescent ink, and were applied for painting and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature phosphorescence
Dual-channel
Detection
Warfarin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Establishing the strategy of CDs@creatine detecting warfarin
Investigation of the selectivity and possible interference
FL and RTP detecting mechanism for warfarin

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Fundamental Research Funds for Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing ( cstc2015zdcy-ztzx120003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Illustration of synthesizing CDs@creatine and their RTP emission mechanism; (B) Mechanism of CDs@creatine detecting warfarin.

(A) FL, (D) RTP spectra and corresponding images of CDs@creatine. Inset: images of CDs@creatine under daylight (a & c) and UV light (b & d); (B) FL and (E) RTP 3D contours of CDs@creatine; (C) FL and (F) RTP decay spectra of CDs@creatine.

(A) HR-TEM images of CDs@creatine; (B) Size distribution of CDs@creatine by DLS; (C) XPS survey of CDs@creatine; XPS spectra of C 1 s (D), N 1 s (E), O 1 s (F) and P 2p (G); (H) FTIR spectrum of CDs@creatine; (I) Absorption and RTP spectra of CDs@creatine.

(A) Images of CDs@creatine painted on paper with heating 1 h at 90 °C under daylight, and with the UV lamp of 365 nm on and off; (B) Images of CDs@creatine for painting a plug, a lamp, a scarecrow and a little boy on paper with heating 1 h at 90 °C under daylight, and with the UV lamp of 365 nm on and off.

(A) FL and (D) RTP intensity and images of CDs@creatine in the absence and presence of warfarin; (B) FL and (E) RTP emission spectra of CDs@creatine with various concentrations of warfarin; Linear relationship of CDs@creatine assying warfarin through FL (C) and RTP (F).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of warfarin and FL spectra of CDs@creatine; (B) RTP lifetime decay of CDs@creatine/warfarin; (C) DSC trace of CDs@creatine/warfarin; (D) FTIR spectra of CDs@creatine/warfarin, CDs@creatine or warfarin, respectively; (E) Illustration of hydrogen bonds forming between CDs@creatine and warfarin.

head.ce:title: Creatine-based carbon dots with room-temperature phosphorescence employed for the dual-channel detection of warfarin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a strategy has been proposed to synthesis carbon dots (CDs@creatine) with blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence based on creatine and phosphoric acid. Subsequently, the dual-channel assaying platform was successfully established. To be specific, the fluorescence of CDs@creatine could be effectively quenched by warfarin through inner filter effect and static quenching effect. Meanwhile, the phosphorescence enhancement of CDs@creatine was attributed to intermolecular hydrogen bonds between CDs@creatine and warfarin. Moreover, the proposed strategy through the phosphorescence signal achieved an improved sensitivity than that with the fluorescence for assaying warfarin.

Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have harvested enormous attention owing to their advanced optical properties, providing their possibility of the wider applications. Here, we designed a facile strategy to synthesize phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots, while creatine provided the carbon and nitrogen source (CDs@creatine). Meanwhile, the as-prepared CDs@creatine exhibited the bright blue fluorescence in aqueous suspension, and showed the green RTP while being fixed on the filter paper. Significantly, the fluorescence of CDs@creatine was quenched by the introduction of warfarin, whereas their RTP was simultaneously enhanced. Specifically, their fluorescence reduction was mainly ascribed to static quenching effect (SQE) and inner filter effect (IFE). In addition, the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs@creatine interacted with the carbonyl groups of warfarin to form hydrogen bonds which rigidified the excited triplet against the non-radiative transition, and thus leading to their enhanced phosphorescence. Therefore, a dual-channel detecting strategy of warfarin has been successfully proposed with the fluorescent linear range from 8 × 10−3 to 8 × 10−7 M and phosphorescent linear range of 2 × 10−5 to 1 × 10−8 M, which established a new way of assaying warfarin. Besides, CDs@creatine served as both fluorescent and phosphorescent ink, and were applied for painting and information encryption.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past decades, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention on the basis of their superior optical property, high biocompatibility and facile preparation. Accordingly, CDs have been remarkably utilized in the fields of fluorescent probes [1,2], imaging [3,4], catalysis [5], optical devices [3,6] and so forth. Simultaneously, diverse synthetic strategies mainly including solvothermal [7-9], microwave-assisted [10,11], thermal pyrolysis [12] and electrochemical exfoliation [13] have been developed to synthesize CDs. In virtue of their superb characteristic of fluorescence (FL), various CDs have been employed to differentiate and detect the targets towards kinds of purpose [3,4].

Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots, as one type of innovative luminescent nano-material emerging, have activated the great interest owing to their attractive luminescence, potentiating their promising applications towards pH sensing [14], information encryption [15,16], anti-counterfeiting [17,18] and beyond. Howbeit, the majority of CDs exhibit the strong fluorescence and barely observable phosphorescence because of inefficient singlet–triplet intersystem crossing (ISC) and unstable triplet excitons easily quenched by oxygen. To achieve the effective RTP on the basis of the emission mechanism, two crucial prerequisites are mainly required. One precondition is to produce triplet excitons, which the CDs possess energy level structure for electron transitions and a small energy gap (ΔEST) between singlet states (S1) and triplet states (T1) to realize efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) [18,19], and the other is that the triplet excitons are protected against non-radiative transitions by specific structure, thus leading to the RTP emerging. Towards the first precondition, it could be effectively accomplished by doping the heteroatoms such as B, N and P into CDs [20,21], which may not only highly promote the spin–orbit coupling (SOC) but also create abundant surface states of CDs. The second one could be acquired by virtue of the assisted matrix [16,22-28] or self-protective structures [20,29] to immobilize CDs, thus obviously promoting the efficient RTP.

Warfarin, as a commonly prescribed drug to prevent the formation of blood clots and their migration in human body, has been widely employed as the effective oral anticoagulant with the appropriate dose [30]. Nevertheless, the long-term or overdose intake of warfarin may increase the risk of bleeding that is severe or life-threatening, especially the patients are prone to bleeding when suffering a disease. Besides, it is hard to propose the appropriate dose of warfarin due to the huge individual difference among patients and its interaction with other drugs. Therefore, monitoring the level of warfarin plays the critical role for obtaining the effective and safe dose of patients [31], and thus developing the ways of detecting warfarin is considered as meaningful.

Herein, we originally prepared one kind of carbon dots by using creatine and phosphoric acid (CDs@creatine) via a microwave treatment, and these CDs displayed not only the bright blue fluorescence but also green room-temperature phosphorescence (Fig. 1 A). To be specific, CDs@creatine merely showed the fluorescence in aqueous suspension, and their RTP was observed by means of CDs@creatine adsorbed onto the filter paper. Significantly, the fluorescence of CDs@creatine was quenched while their phosphorescence was enhanced by introducing warfarin (Fig. 1B). Meanwhile, we investigated the mechanism of warfarin suppressing the fluorescence and enhancing the phosphorescence of CDs@creatine. Likewise, the fluorescence decrease of CDs@creatine was mainly resulted from SQE and IFE, while the hydrogen bonds forming between CDs@creatine and warfarin facilitated the intersystem crossing (ISC) process as well as stabilized the triplet state excitons at room temperature, thus enhancing their phosphorescence.

Creatine and warfarin sodium were purchased from bidepharm (Shanghai, China), and N, N-dimethylformamide, phosphoric acid, L-threonine, L-cysteine, L-arginine, melamine, metal ions (Cu+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ba2+, Sr2+, K+) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Similarly, heparin sodium salt, phytic acid, 4-acetamidophenol, L-glutathione and nicotinamide were acquired from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Also, L-alanine, L-histidine, D-glucose, ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide, acetic acid and ethyl acetate were purchased from Chron Chemicals (Chengdu, China). Again, the ultrapure water of 18.25 MΩ cm was supplied by an Aquapro AWL-0502-P ultrapure water system (Chongqing, China), while Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer was utilized to regulate the pH values of suspension during the whole experimental process.

All the fluorescence and phosphorescence measurements were performed through a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), and the UV–vis absorptions were carried out by a Shimadzu UV-1750 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Subsequently, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained with a TECANI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) at 200 KV, while the dynamic light scattering (DLS) data were acquired by Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, England). Next, the elements and functional groups analyses were separately achieved by a ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan) and a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). Also, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired with a Rigaku Ministar instrument (Tokyo, Japan). Besides, the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetime of CDs@creatine were measured at room-temperature through a HORIBA FL-3 spectrometer (Paris, France), and their quantum yield was measured by an absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer C11347 (Hamamatsu, Japan). Again, the centrifuge (TG16-W) of Cence instrument Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China) was applied to separate the mixtures. Then, the powder of CDs@creatine was obtained by PiloFD8-4.3 V (Charlotte, USA) through lyophilization, and the water bath of DF-101 s from Gongyi experimental instruments factory (Gongyi, China) was introduced to the detection procedures. Furthermore, a commercial microwave oven of Galanz P70D20TL-D4 was employed for preparing CDs@creatine.

In a typical synthesis, 1.25 g of creatine was initially introduced into 4.0 mL of ultrapure water followed by dropwise adding 1.0 mL of phosphoric acid. After stirring, the formed transparent mixture was transferred into a crucible and heated by a domestic oven of 560 W for 75 s. Subsequently, the crucible was cooled down to room temperature, and the rude product was collected and dissolved into 20 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the pH of the previous suspension was adjusted to 7 with the NaOH solution, and the product obtained was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 8 min to remove the agglomerated or large particles. Again, the supernatant was subjected to the filtration with 0.22 μm of filter membrane to separate with the undissolved particles. Finally, the filtrate was acquired by dialysis (MWCO: 500 Da) for 7 h, and the powder of CDs@creatine was obtained through lyophilization.

In brief, 100 μL of CDs@creatine (10 mg mL−1) and 100 μL of warfarin solution (0.01 M) were successively added into 1.5 mL of Eppendorf (EP) tube, and then diluted to 1 mL with 800 μL of BR buffer solution (pH = 8). Next, this mixture was incubated at 50 °C for 10 min, and their fluorescence intensity was measured with the excitation of 344 nm as well as the phosphorescence was recorded at the excitation of 322 nm.

Routinely, the current CDs@creatine were prepared via a microwave-assisted treatment based on creatine and phosphoric acid. Briefly, we employed creatine as the carbon and nitrogen source, since the presence of C = N and C = O with lone-pair electrons favored the n-π* transition and hence facilitated the emerging of ISC from S1 to T1 [32,33]. To identify the as-prepared CDs@creatine, their fluorescence spectra were initially obtained. More specifically, CDs@creatine emitted the blue fluorescence of 394 nm with the excitation of 344 nm (Fig. 2 A). Meanwhile, their 3D fluorescence plots of CDs@creatine exhibited the strongest emission peak centered at 394 nm with the excitation wavelength ranging from 300 nm to 380 nm (Fig. 2B). Moreover, CDs@creatine showed an average fluorescence lifetime of 4.24 ns (Fig. 2C), and their fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs@creatine was calibrated to be 24.7% according to the reference of quinine sulfate (Fig. S1). To clarify the RTP emission of CDs@creatine, the filter paper was introduced to immobilize CDs@creatine. Interestingly, CDs@creatine coated on the filter paper exhibited the green RTP at 480 nm with the excitation of 322 nm (Fig. 2D). As shown in Fig. 2E, their phosphorescence could be excited by the wavelengths from 240 nm to 360 nm with the optimum emission of 480 nm by the excitation of 322 nm. Also, their average phosphorescence lifetime obtained was 1.06 μs (Fig. 2F), and the phosphorescence quantum yield of CDs@creatine coated on filter paper was measured as 7.34%. However, CDs@creatine did not show the phosphorescence in the presence of water.

To understand the origin of the optical property of CDs@creatine, their morphology and structure were initially analyzed. In particular, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of CDs@creatine showed a lattice fringe of 0.22 nm (Fig. 3 A), which was related to the planes of graphene (100) [34]. And the XRD pattern of CDs@creatine displayed two major peaks at 14.9° and 27.7° (Fig. S2), respectively corresponding to the 100 and 002 facets of graphene [35,36]. Also, the diameter of CDs@creatine obtained by DLS ranged from 5.0 nm to 9.7 nm (Fig. 3B). Subsequently, the chemical composition and functional groups of CDs@creatine were identified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR). The results of XPS indicated that CDs@creatine mainly consisted of C (41.55%), N (23.12%), O (27.02%) and P (8.31%) (Fig. 3C). For C 1 s, the curve was fitted into three peaks at 284.2 eV, 285.2 eV and 287.0 eV, which were originated from C = C, C-N, and C = O, respectively (Fig. 3D). Likewise, N 1 s curve exhibited the presence of N atoms in the form of C-N-C (397.6 eV), N-(C)3/N-P (398.9 eV), and amide N (399.6 eV) (Fig. 3E). Meanwhile, the O 1 s survey was deconvoluted into three peaks at 530.0 eV, 531.5 eV, and 532.4 eV, which were ascribed to P = O, C-OH/C-O-C, and P-O, respectively (Fig. 3F). Again, there appeared two peaks of 132.8 eV (P-O) and 133.9 eV (P-N/P-C) for P 2p (Fig. 3G).

The FTIR spectrum of CDs@creatine was recorded to gain insight into their functional groups (Fig. 3H). Particularly, the FTIR spectrum peaks at 1648 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C = O and C-N, while the FTIR peaks at 1093 cm−1 and 881 cm−1 referred to P-O-C and N-P, respectively. Again, the broad peak around 3100–3600 cm−1 described the presence of O-H and N-H on their surface. Taken together, these results of FTIR and XPS were consistent, suggesting that CDs@creatine abounded with the crucial functional groups of C = O/C-N, facilitating their RTP emission [18,20].

In addition, the absorption and RTP excitation-emission spectra of CDs@creatine were performed (Fig. 3I). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs@creatine showed two absorption peaks at 224 nm and 320 nm, which were ascribed to the π-π* transition of C = C and n-π* transition of C = O, respectively [18,34]. Meanwhile, the phosphorescence excitation centered at 322 nm overlapped with the absorption band of C = O (Fig. 3I), revealing that C = O was responsible for their RTP emission of CDs@creatine.

To obtain the optimal synthesis conditions, the detailed experiments were designed and conducted. As shown in Fig. S3A and S3B, the phosphorescence intensity of CDs@creatine varied with the reaction time and diverse mass of creatine, and 75 s of reaction time and 1.25 g of creatine were identified as the optimized condition toward synthesizing CDs@creatine. Next, different microwave power was employed to prepare CDs@creatine with their phosphorescence intensity followed by measuring with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the optimum phosphorescence was obtained with 560 W (Figure S3C). Then, various volume ratios of phosphoric acid and ultrapure water were introduced, and 1 mL of phosphoric acid and 4 mL of ultrapure water were identified as the optimized condition (Figure S3D). Therefore, 1.25 g of creatine and 1 mL of phosphoric acid dissolved in 4 mL of ultrapure water with heating by microwave at 560 W for 75 s were selected as the optimized conditions toward synthesizing CDs@creatine.

To explore the FL stability of CDs@creatine, the possible influence of time, temperature, pH, salt ion concentrations, organic solvents and metal ions on their fluorescence intensity were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S4A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@creatine scarcely varied after irradiated by UV lamp of 365 nm for 80 min, and keep stable as the temperature increasing from 20 to 80 ℃ (Fig. S4B). Also, different concentrations of NaCl (0.01–1 M) showed little effect on their fluorescence (Fig. S4D), demonstrating the salt-resistant characteristic of CDs@creatine. Additionally, the selected organic solvents and metal ions showed little impact on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@creatine (Fig. S4E and S4F). These findings demonstrated the generally satisfactory fluorescence stability of CDs@creatine. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@creatine increased with the pH varying from 2 to 13, which was resulted from the protonation-deprotonation process of hydroxyl groups or amino groups on their surface (Fig. S4C).

Interestingly, the obvious green RTP was observed when CDs@creatine were coated on the filter paper and heated at 90 ℃ for 1 h. To clarify the mechanism, the molecules of bulk water evaporated during the heating process, and the filter paper promoted the rigidity of CDs@creatine by the hydrogen bonds forming between CDs@creatine and the filter paper. Simultaneously, the nitrogen atoms doped highly encouraged the spin–orbit coupling, and facilitated the ISC to populate numerous triplet state excitons. Taken together, the non-radiative decay was restricted and abundant triplet state excitons existed on the surface of CDs@creatine, thus the green RTP emission emerging.

Basically, the green RTP of CDs@creatine lasted up to 8 s once the UV lamp was turned off (Fig. 4 A). Encouraged by their attractive dual-channel luminescence properties and good repeatability, we employed CDs@creatine as RTP ink for graphic painting. As shown in Fig. 4B, all the patterns exhibited the pale-yellow color with daylight, and their bright blue FL signals were clearly observed under UV lamp, while the green RTP were distinctly recognized along with the shutdown of UV lamp. This application potentiated CDs@creatine as the favorable fluorescent and phosphorescent ink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing the strategy of CDs@creatine detecting warfarin
ce:section.ce:para: By virtue of their excellent dual-channel emission of FL and RTP, we thus addressed whether CDs@creatine could serve as a sensor or not. Fortunately, both the fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs@creatine responded to warfarin. In brief, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@creatine decreased by adding 0.01 M warfarin (Fig. 5 A), while the phosphorescence increased by introducing 1 μM warfarin (Fig. 5D), suggesting that CDs@creatine showed the potential of detecting warfarin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the selectivity and possible interference
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the selectivity of CDs@creatine determining warfarin, the corresponding experiments were designed and performed. Particularly, 100 μL of CDs@creatine (10 mg mL−1) and 100 μL of each analogue compound (Vitamin C, erythromycin, acetaminophen, D(-)-penicillamine, glucose, metformin, melamine, L-cysteine, lactose, L-arginine with the concentration of 10−2 M) were parallelly added into 1.5 mL of EP tubes, and then diluted to 1 mL with 800 μL of BR buffer solution (pH = 8). These results suggested good selectivity of CDs@creatine for warfarin (Fig. S5A). Similarly, the potential effect of other chemicals and ions (each of 10-2 M) on the current detection was investigated. Likewise, 100 μL of CDs@creatine (10 mg mL−1), 700 μL of BR buffer (pH = 8), 100 μL of warfarin (10−1 M) and 100 μL of various substance (L-histidine, L-glycine, Mn2+, glyphosine, heparin sodium, L-alanine, phytic acid, GSH, L-threonine) were introduced into the parallel EP tubes respectively, and these substances implied scarce impact on the detection procedures (Fig. S5B).

To optimize the detecting conditions of warfarin, the factors that might influence the detection were explored. Initially, various pH conditions were introduced, and the fluorescence decrease reached the maximum with pH = 8 (Fig. S6A). Subsequently, the possible effect by the reaction temperature and time were studied. Consequently, 50 °C and 10 min were identified as the optimal conditions for detecting warfarin (Fig. S6B and S6C).

The strategy of detecting warfarin was thereby successfully established based on the previous optimized conditions. To be specific, various concentrations of warfarin (10 nM to 8 mM) were separately added into CDs@creatine under the optimized conditions. Importantly, the fluorescence decreased with the increasing amount of warfarin ranging from 0.8 μM to 8.0 mM (R2 = 0.986), and the detection limit of was determined to be 0.33 mM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 5B and 5C). Meanwhile, the phosphorescence was enhanced by the introduction of varied concentrations of warfarin from 10 nM to 20 μM (R2 = 0.973) with the detection limit of 9.33 nM (Fig. 5E and 5F). Obviously, the proposed detection strategy through the RTP signal achieved an improved sensitivity than that through the FL signal for assaying warfarin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL and RTP detecting mechanism for warfarin
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs@creatine by warfarin was discussed. Initially, we explored whether the quenched fluorescence was by dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or static quenching effect (SQE), that could be theoretically described by Stern-Volmer equations of dynamic quenching (F0/F = 1 + KSV [Q]) and static quenching (F0/F = 1 + kq [Q]). Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CDs@creatine without and with warfarin, and KSV is Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] represents the concentration of warfarin. In this study, the linear relationship between F0/F and different concentrations of warfarin was obtained (Fig. 5C), thus F0/F = 0.50093 [Q] – 0.09009, R2 = 0.986. For DQE, KSV = kq × τ0, where kq is the biomolecular annihilation constant as well as τ0 for the fluorescence lifetime of CDs@creatine without warfarin. For CDs@creatine, KSV = 0.50093 mM and τ0 = 4.24 ns, and kq was therewith calculated to be 1.18 × 1011 M−1 s−1, that was obviously higher than the typical quenching constant of 1.0–2.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1 [9,37]. Hence, these results could exclude the possibility of DQE, and SQE was accrodingly responsible for their fluorescence quenching of CDs@creatine rather than DQE.

Usually, inner filter effect (IFE) could efficiently happen when the absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the fluorophore [38]. As described in Fig. 6 A, the absorption spectrum of warfarin overlapped with the fluorescence excitation of CDs@creatine, demonstrating that the fluorescence decrease was associated with IFE. Taken together, both SQE and IFE dominated the fluorescence quenching of CDs@creatine by warfarin.

Meanwhile, we found that the RTP of CDs@creatine/warfarin obviously showed the stronger intensity than that of CDs@creatine (Fig. 5D), and the RTP lifetime of CDs@creatine increased to 604.47 ms once warfarin was introduced (Fig. 6B). To elucidate the mechanism of the enhanced phosphorescence of CDs@creatine, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was further employed. In particular, CDs@creatine showed an endothermic transition at 70.91 °C during the heating phase due to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds breaking down (Fig. 6C), demonstrating the existence of hydrogen bonds [20,39]. Consequently, for CDs@creatine, there existed ample hydroxyl groups which preferred to form hydrogen bonds with the carbonyl and hydroxyl groups of warfarin (O-H…O-H, O-H…O = C), and thereby the rigid hydrogen-bonded networks improved the stability of CDs@creatine/warfarin, and promoted to reduce the non-radiative transition of the triplet excitons.

To further understand the enhanced phosphorescence, the structures of CDs@creatine and warfarin were investigated. As shown in the FTIR of CDs@creatine (Fig. 6D), the absorption band at 3600 cm−1 corresponded to ν(O-H), and this peak and the band around 3300 cm−1 ν(N-H) merged into a broader and stronger peak at 3200–3400 cm−1 after introducing warfarin, revealing the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between CDs@creatine and warfarin. Thus, the formation of hydrogen bonds was further supported by the FTIR results. Overall, the hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of CDs@creatine interacted with warfarin and formed hydrogen bonds, leading to the appearance of the non-fluorescent CDs@creatine/warfarin (Fig. 6E). Simultaneously, the formed hydrogen bonds facilitated the stability of the triplet states, thus enhancing their phosphorescence of CDs@creatine.

In summary, we here designed a convenient and cost-effective strategy to prepare the RTP carbon dots through a microwave treatment. Specifically, the as-prepared CDs@creatine showed the blue fluorescence under 360 nm of UV light, while the obvious green phosphorescence of CDs@creatine emerged when being coated on the filter paper with UV off at room temperature. Meanwhile, CDs@creatine showed the potentiality of serving as the phosphorescent ink. Importantly, the introduction of warfarin resulted in the fluorescence quenching and phosphorescence enhancement of CDs@creatine, providing an innovative approach to detecting warfarin. This established detection strategy demonstrated that the RTP signal of CDs@creatine was more sensitive than FL signal towards detecting warfarin. In addition, the mechanism for assaying warfarin have been attributed to the hydrogen bonds formed between CDs@creatine and warfarin, which facilitated their FL quenching and RTP enhancing. Consequently, CDs@creatine provided a new dual-channel strategy of detecting warfarin.

Miao Fu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing-original draft, Investigation. Zhiying Feng: Software, Validation. Jing Wang: Review & editing. Ying Zhu: Review & editing. Lanlan Gan: Resources. Xiaoming Yang: Conceptualization, Writing-review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Fundamental Research Funds for Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing (cstc2015zdcy-ztzx120003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.151298.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High quantum yield boron-doped carbon dots: a ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of Mg2+ ions
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.K. Sadhanala, S. Pagidi and A. Gedanken, High quantum yield boron-doped carbon dots: a ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of Mg2+ ions, J. Mater. Chem. C 9 (2021) 1632-1640.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A red emissive two-photon fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for intracellular pH detection
Small

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.X. Ye, Y.H. Xiang, Q.R. Wang, Z. Li, and Z.H. Liu, A red emissive two-photon fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for intracellular pH detection, Small 15 (2019) 1901673.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright hydrophilic and organophilic fluorescence carbon dots: one-pot fabrication and multi-functional applications at visualized Au3+ detection in cell and white light-emitting devices
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.L. Gao, Y.M. Zhou, C.G. Xu, M.X. Guo, Z.C. Qi, X.J. Peng and B. Gao, Bright hydrophilic and organophilic fluorescence carbon dots: one-pot fabrication and multi-functional applications at visualized Au3+ detection in cell and white light-emitting devices, Sens.
